episode_meta.title: Craig Newmark: Philanthropy's Role in Supporting Journalism in the AI Era

use_cases.description: Applying large language models to customer service, with committed customer service reps updating models when they fail to solve problems, evolving the reps' role into training LLMs
use_cases.outcome: Customer service systems far superior to current standard; customer service reps evolve into better-paid LLM training roles

use_cases.description: Common Sense Media developing guidelines and rating sites that use large language models for suitability for children, similar to their existing TV and movie reviews
use_cases.outcome: Parents and users can rely on ratings and guidelines for AI-powered sites regarding child safety

use_cases.description: Using Wikipedia's reliable sources and deprecated sources framework as a model for training trustworthy large language models
use_cases.outcome: Proposed framework for ensuring LLMs are trained on reliable rather than deprecated sources

use_cases.description: Craig GPT project: feeding everything Craig Newmark has written into a GPT system to create a fact-checking tool for his own statements
use_cases.outcome: Initially a gag for veterans fundraising; potential evolution into a personal fact-checking system

use_cases.description: Journalists using AI to do research, filter important signals out of noise, connect the dots in huge amounts of data, and get suggested writing assistance
use_cases.outcome: Enhanced research capability, data analysis, and writing assistance for journalists

use_cases.description: CUNY AI Journalism Lab teaching journalism professionals and students about generative AI, prompt engineering, and what to use and avoid
use_cases.outcome: AI literacy for journalists; applications open at time of recording

key_claims.claim: If you could write systems which understood natural language you would be far on your way towards understanding consciousness
key_claims.speaker: Craig Newmark

key_claims.claim: In the early 1970s, close to no one was interested in natural language understanding and neural networks, and there were not going to be any jobs available for decades
key_claims.speaker: Craig Newmark

key_claims.claim: If you apply AI and large language models to customer service seriously and keep models up to date, you might build customer service systems far superior to what most companies do
key_claims.speaker: Craig Newmark

key_claims.claim: A trustworthy press is the immune system of democracy
key_claims.speaker: Craig Newmark (attributing to his high school history teacher Mr. Scholsky)

key_claims.claim: The internet and everything associated with it has evolved faster than law, regulation, and custom can keep up with, and AI is doing that even more
key_claims.speaker: Craig Newmark

key_claims.claim: OpenAI's Sora video generation tool surprised Newmark; he thought that capability was two or three years off
key_claims.speaker: Craig Newmark

key_claims.claim: A movie or TV script is a program, software, that could be fed into generative AI at some point to produce something credible
key_claims.speaker: Craig Newmark

key_claims.claim: No one has the answer for trustworthy journalism and trustworthy large language models because hardly anyone is thinking about the problem
key_claims.speaker: Craig Newmark

key_claims.claim: CUNY has been about helping people who grow up with little or no money get good educations for nearly 200 years
key_claims.speaker: Craig Newmark

challenges.description: Not enough people are thinking about guardrails for GPT systems intended for children
Large language models can hallucinate or lie to people, and training them on reliable sources is an unsolved problem
No one has the answer for trustworthy journalism combined with trustworthy large language models because hardly anyone is thinking about the problem
The internet and AI evolve faster than law, regulation, and custom can keep up with
Journalists reporting on AI need significant technical background to distinguish hype versus reality
Trust and safety teams want to do the right thing but face all sorts of pressure
AI advances are outpacing expectations, with tools like Sora arriving years earlier than anticipated, creating reactive rather than proactive responses
AI's impact on entertainment industry is moving faster than writers, directors, and studios anticipated

quotes.text: I'm thinking you put together large networks of simulated neurons, and you can understand language. And maybe, like in some of the stories I've read, consciousness might even spontaneously emerge.
quotes.speaker: Craig Newmark
quotes.topic: Early theoretical interest in neural networks and consciousness in the 1970s

quotes.text: A trustworthy press is the immune system of democracy. We need a trustworthy press speaking truth to power, asking tough questions, to survive as a democracy.
quotes.speaker: Craig Newmark
quotes.topic: Role of journalism in democracy and motivation for philanthropic support

quotes.text: We need very few if any hallucinations. Which is a polite way of talking about the large language model going off the rails and lying to people.
quotes.speaker: Craig Newmark
quotes.topic: LLM hallucinations and the need for trustworthy generative AI in journalism

quotes.text: Journalists need to get right to the point in terms of the capabilities and limitations of AI as it exists right now. But also need to think about the rate at which generative AI is evolving, because people keep surprising us, shocking us with actual progress.
quotes.speaker: Craig Newmark
quotes.topic: Journalistic responsibility in covering AI accurately

quotes.text: I think journalists can use AI to do research to filter important signals out of noise. For example, if you have huge amounts of data, sometimes generative AI can connect the dots and make sense out of huge amounts of data.
quotes.speaker: Craig Newmark
quotes.topic: Practical AI use cases for journalists

quotes.text: We need philanthropists to get involved hands-on, focusing on trustworthy reporting with generative AI that's been trained with guardrails.
quotes.speaker: Craig Newmark
quotes.topic: Philanthropic support for responsible AI in journalism

recommendations.advice: Journalism schools should teach students and alumni about generative AI, including prompt engineering, what to use, and what to avoid
recommendations.speaker: Craig Newmark

recommendations.advice: Use Wikipedia's reliable sources and deprecated sources framework as a starting point for evaluating what large language models should be trained on
recommendations.speaker: Craig Newmark
recommendations.target_audience: AI developers, trust and safety teams

recommendations.advice: Philanthropists should support trustworthy journalism and get involved hands-on, focusing on trustworthy reporting with generative AI trained with guardrails
recommendations.speaker: Craig Newmark
recommendations.target_audience: Philanthropists and people with discretionary cash

recommendations.advice: Put guardrails around GPT systems intended for children; defer to Common Sense Media for child safety guidelines and site ratings
recommendations.speaker: Craig Newmark

recommendations.advice: Journalists reporting on AI must have significant technical background to distinguish hype versus reality and understand the capabilities and limitations of current AI
recommendations.speaker: Craig Newmark

recommendations.advice: Customer service representatives should evolve their roles from doing straight customer service to training and updating large language models
recommendations.speaker: Craig Newmark

external_references: CUNY Graduate School of Journalism (City University of New York)
Wikipedia reliable sources and deprecated sources
Columbia University Center for Journalism Ethics and Security
University of Washington Center for an Informed Public
Harvard Berkman-Klein Center for Internet and Society
